note_data.title: Chemical Bonding
keyPoints: **Ionic bonding** occurs between metals and non-metals through transfer of electrons from metal atoms to non-metal atoms
**Covalent bonding** involves sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms
**Dative covalent bonding** forms when one atom provides both electrons in a shared pair
**Metallic bonding** involves electrostatic attraction between positive metal ions and delocalised electrons
**Electronegativity** is the ability of an atom to attract bonding electrons in a covalent bond
**VSEPR theory** predicts molecular shapes based on electron pair repulsion around central atom
**Van der Waals forces** are weak intermolecular forces including London dispersion forces and permanent dipole interactions
**Hydrogen bonding** occurs between hydrogen bonded to N, O, or F and lone pairs on N, O, or F atoms
**Bond polarity** depends on electronegativity difference between bonded atoms

definitions.definition: The electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer
The electrostatic attraction between two nuclei and a shared pair of electrons
A covalent bond in which one atom provides both electrons in the shared pair
The ability of an atom to attract bonding electrons in a covalent bond
Weak intermolecular forces of attraction between molecules

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Use VSEPR theory to determine shape based on electron pairs
Give similarities and differences with clear justification

modelAnswer.question: Explain why ammonia (NH₃) has a pyramidal shape and predict the bond angle. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Nitrogen has 4 electron pairs around it (3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair). The 4 electron pairs arrange themselves tetrahedrally to minimise repulsion. However, the molecular shape is pyramidal because only the bonding pairs determine the shape. The bond angle is less than 109.5° (approximately 107°) because lone pair-bonding pair repulsion is greater than bonding pair-bonding pair repulsion.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Pyramidal shape due to lone pair
Mark 3: Bond angle less than 109.5° due to lone pair repulsion

commonMistakes: Never say 'electrons are shared equally' in polar covalent bonds — always specify 'unequally shared'
Students often confuse molecular shape with electron pair geometry — shape depends only on bonding pairs
Never say 'molecules have ionic bonding' — ionic bonding exists between ions, not molecules
Students often forget that dative bonds are shown with arrows (→) not lines in diagrams
Never confuse intermolecular forces with intramolecular bonds — they are different types of attractions
examinerTips: Bond angles appear frequently in Paper 2 — always relate to electron pair repulsion
Draw dot-and-cross diagrams clearly showing outer electrons only for full marks
VSEPR questions worth 3-4 marks appear almost every year
Always state the number of electron pairs before predicting molecular shape
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: VSEPR shapes diagram showing linear (2 pairs), trigonal planar (3 pairs), tetrahedral (4 pairs), trigonal bipyramidal (5 pairs), and octahedral (6 pairs) arrangements with bond angles labeled
